description:
This image presents a side-by-side comparison of two satellite imagery types: Radar and Optical, over a geographical region featuring mountainous terrain, dense vegetation, and an intricate river delta/estuary system.

The left image, labelled "Radar", is a grayscale Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) image. It distinctly shows detailed topography, including rugged mountainous terrain in the upper half, and complex water body patterns like a winding river network and coastal estuaries in the lower half. The vertical axis is labelled "azimuth", and the horizontal axis is labelled "range", which defines the imaging geometry. A small icon representing a satellite emitting a radar beam is positioned at the intersection of the azimuth and range axes.

The right image, labelled "Optical", is a natural-colour satellite image of the same or a similar area. It shows dense green vegetation and dark water bodies but is significantly obscured by extensive white cumulus clouds, particularly over the vegetated and coastal areas. A larger, lighter brown cloud bank is visible near the center.

The comparison highlights the primary advantage of radar imagery, which is its ability to acquire surface information regardless of cloud cover, in contrast to optical imagery where ground visibility is often obstructed by atmospheric conditions.
